attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-day history of three episodes of painless vision loss in his right eye. Each episode lasted approximately two minutes and he reported he covered one eye when it occurred. His vision returned to normal after each episode. He has a medical history of hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia, and is an ex-smoker with a 40 pack-year history. His medications include amlodipine and atorvastatin. He played competitive football as an adult with multiple previous concussions, but denies any recent head trauma. There was no associated vomiting or headaches.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 158/92 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.6°C. Visual acuity is 6/6 in both eyes. Fundoscopic examination reveals normal optic discs and retinal vessels. A carotid bruit is audible on the right side.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of amaurosis fugax - transient monocular vision loss described often as a curtain-like effect. The presence of vascular risk factors (hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia, smoking history) and a carotid bruit strongly support amaurosis fugax as the diagnosis. This condition typically occurs due to temporary retinal ischaemia from emboli originating from carotid artery disease. It can be considered a type of transient ischemic attack and thus any ischaemic risk factors should be managed appropriately, and to rule out atrial fibrillation. Carotid doppler ultrasound, electrocardiography, holter monitor, full blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, lipid profile, and blood glucose.

Retinal migraine typically affects younger patients and is often accompanied by headache or other migraine symptoms. The visual disturbance is usually described as scintillating scotomas rather than a curtain-like effect, and more likely to be binocular visual loss.

Giant cell arteritis typically presents in patients over 50 years with headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and constitutional symptoms. While it can cause vision loss, the loss is usually permanent rather than transient.

Retinal detachment typically presents with persistent visual field defects, often described as a curtain or shadow, but these are permanent rather than transient. Patients may also report flashing lights or floaters.

Optic neuritis typically presents with subacute vision loss over days, often with pain on eye movement. It is more common in younger patients and is often associated with multiple sclerosis.
learningPoints: Amaurosis fugax typically presents as a transient monocular vision loss lasting minutes, described as a curtain falling across the visual field.
Amaurosis fugax is commonly caused by emboli from carotid artery disease and requires urgent investigation for stroke prevention.
Other causes of transient vision loss include migraine, giant cell arteritis, and retinal vasospasm.
references: Martinez-Viguera A et al. Clinical characteristics and outcome of amaurosis fugax due to transient retinal ischemia: Results from a contemporary cohort.  Journal of Stroke and Cerebrovascular Diseases. 2023.  Available at: https://www.strokejournal.org/article/S1052-3057(23)00358-0/pdf

Jeffery R, Chen F, Lueck C. Blackout: Understanding transient vision loss.  Australian Journal of General PRactice.  2021: 50 (3).  Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2021/march/blackout-understanding-transient-vision-loss
